function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Amazon's FY2025 10-K discloses AWS revenue of $128.7 billion and total company capex of $131.8 billion, predominantly directed toward AWS and AI infrastructure. SpaceX's S-1 discloses AI segment revenue of $3,201 million for FY2025 and AI segment capex of $7,723 million for Q1 2026 alone. Calculate capex-to-revenue ratios for AWS (using consolidated Amazon capex as a proxy) and SpaceX's AI segment (using annualized Q1 2026 capex). What does the comparison imply about the relative stage of infrastructure buildout versus monetization maturity, and what structural difference in each entity's compute-business model explains why a direct multiple comparison is analytically incomplete?

steps.reasoning_content:

1. I need to verify Amazon's FY2025 10-K figures: AWS revenue of $128.7 billion and total company capex of $131.8 billion
2. I need to verify SpaceX's S-1 figures: AI segment revenue of $3,201 million for FY2025 and AI segment capex of $7,723 million for Q1 2026
3. Calculate capex-to-revenue ratios for both
4. Analyze what the comparison implies about infrastructure buildout vs monetization maturity
5. Explain structural differences in compute-business models

Let me start by retrieving the SpaceX S-1 filing to verify the AI segment figures, and also search for Amazon's 10-K to verify those figures.

*   **Total Company Capital Expenditures:** 
    *   **Total net additions to property and equipment** (which includes non-cash activity such as equipment acquired but not yet paid): **$142,352 million**

Based on the provided financial statements and MD&A excerpts, here are the details regarding the AI segment's capital expenditures and revenue. 

*Note: The provided text only contains **Full-Year** and **First Quarter (Q1)** data. A complete Q2–Q4 quarterly breakdown or a full-year breakdown by revenue type is not available in the provided excerpts.*

### 1. AI Segment Capital Expenditures (CapEx)
**Details & Strategic Drivers:**
The massive surge in AI capital expenditures is primarily driven by the company's strategy to prioritize growth and capture opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure. Specifically, the investments are funding the **rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers**, which includes the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure (such as GPU hardware and cloud computing infrastructure). The segment is described as being in an "earlier stage of development," requiring heavy upfront CapEx to support long-term growth.

**CapEx by Year and Available Quarters (in millions):**
| Period | AI Capital Expenditures | Year-over-Year / Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Year 2023** | $463 | Baseline year |
| **Full Year 2024** | $5,633 | Massive increase driven by data center build-outs |
| **Full Year 2025** | $12,727 | Continued aggressive infrastructure expansion |
| **Q1 2025** *(3 months ended Mar 31)* | $2,567 | — |
| **Q1 2026** *(3 months ended Mar 31)* | $7,723 | Increased by **$5,156M** vs Q1 2025 |

---

### 2. AI Segment Revenue Breakdown
**Annual Revenue Totals:**
While the text does not provide a categorical breakdown (e.g., Advertising vs. API) for the full years, it does provide the total AI segment revenue by year:
*   **2023:** $2,961 million
*   **2024:** $2,620 million
*   **2025:** $3,201 million

**Quarterly Revenue Breakdown by Type (Q1 Only):**
The text provides a specific revenue breakdown by type for the first quarters of 2025 and 2026. The $91 million year-over-year increase in Q1 2026 was primarily driven by higher X and Grok subscriptions.
| Revenue Type | Q1 2025 (3 Months) | Q1 2026 (3 Months) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Advertising | $443 million | $343 million |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure | $284 million | $475 million |
| **Total AI Revenue** | **$727 million** | **$818 million** |

**Revenue Streams & Recognition Policies:**
The AI segment generates revenue through three primary channels, which are recognized as follows:
1.  **Subscriptions:** X Premium and xAI API subscriptions (recognized ratably over the subscription term, ranging from month-to-month to one year).
2.  **Data Licensing:** Licensing data to customers (recognized ratably over the period the customer consumes and benefits from the data, up to two years).
3.  **API Access:** Providing API access to Grok models (recognized ratably for stand-ready access, or as services are consumed for usage-based arrangements).

arguments.prompt: What are the detailed capital expenditure figures including purchases of property and equipment, cash paid for property and equipment, and any other capex-related line items from the cash flow statement for FY2025 in {{amazon_10k_2025}}?
What is the AI segment full year 2025 capital expenditure and what is the description of the AI segment's stage of development and infrastructure buildout in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial disclosures for FY2025, here are the detailed capital expenditure figures and related line items:

### **1. Purchases of Property and Equipment (Cash Outflows)**
*   **Purchases of property and equipment:** **$(131,819) million** (reported as a cash outflow in the Investing Activities section of the Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows).

### **2. Cash Paid for Property and Equipment (Cash Capital Expenditures)**
*   **Cash capital expenditures:** **$128.3 billion** (highlighted in the MD&A Liquidity and Capital Resources section).
*   **Purchases of property and equipment, net of proceeds from sales and incentives:** **$(128,320) million** (reported in the Free Cash Flow reconciliation table). This figure represents the actual cash paid for CapEx after accounting for vendor incentives and asset sales.

### **3. Other CapEx-Related Line Items (Investing Activities)**
*   **Proceeds from property and equipment sales and incentives:** **$3,499 million** (cash inflow offsetting gross purchases in the Investing Activities section).

### **4. Supplemental & Non-Cash CapEx Information**
*   **Property and equipment acquired under finance leases, net of remeasurements and modifications:** **$2,911 million** (non-cash investing activity).
*   **Increase in property and equipment acquired but not yet paid:** **$10,155 million** (non-cash investing activity representing CapEx accrued but not yet disbursed in cash).
*   **Property and equipment acquired but not yet paid (Ending Balance):** **$27.0 billion** (recorded within "Accounts payable" on the balance sheet as of December 31, 2025).
*   **Assets acquired under operating leases:** **$19,930 million** (non-cash supplemental figure related to capacity expansion, though technically classified as operating leases rather than owned CapEx).

### **5. Total Net Additions to Property and Equipment**
Total net additions (which include technology infrastructure assets and the effect of non-cash activity such as property and equipment acquired but not yet paid) totaled **$142,352 million** for the consolidated company. The breakdown by segment is as follows:
*   **AWS:** $96,496 million *(includes $1.9 billion under finance leases and $421 million under build-to-suit arrangements)*
*   **North America:** $35,919 million *(includes $1.0 billion under finance leases and $20 million under build-to-suit arrangements)*
*   **International:** $7,617 million
*   **Corporate:** $2,320 million

Based on the provided financial overview, here are the details regarding the AI segment:

**Full Year 2025 Capital Expenditure**
* The AI segment's capital expenditures for the full year ended December 31, 2025, were **$12,727 million** (an increase of $7,094 million compared to 2024). 

**Stage of Development**
* **Earlier Stage & Nascent Market:** The segment is described as being in an "earlier stage of development" and operating in a "nascent and rapidly evolving market" where the long-term commercial value proposition of frontier AI models remains largely unproven.
* **Multi-Year Investment Horizon:** The company expects a "multi-year investment horizon" characterized by significant operating losses before its massive deployments translate into sustained positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA. The company is intentionally prioritizing execution speed, capacity expansion, and technological leadership over near-term margin optimization.

**Infrastructure Buildout**
* **Terrestrial Data Centers:** The massive surge in capital expenditures is primarily driven by significant investments in the "rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure."
* **Orbital Data Centers:** The company also outlines future plans to "launch orbital data centers" (leveraging its Starship launch vehicle to reduce the cost of reaching orbit and build out space-based infrastructure).
* **Aggressive Scaling:** During this investment period, the company plans to allocate substantial capital to expand its compute infrastructure, noting that capital expenditures "will scale as quickly as we are able to deploy power and compute" to capture a $26.5 trillion potential market opportunity.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me compile the analysis:

**Amazon (FY2025):**
- AWS Revenue: $128,725 million ($128.7 billion)
- Total Company Capex (purchases of property and equipment from cash flow statement): $131,819 million ($131.8 billion)
- AWS-specific capex: $96,496 million
- The question says to use consolidated Amazon capex as a proxy

**SpaceX AI Segment:**
- FY2025 AI segment revenue: $3,201 million
- Q1 2026 AI segment capex: $7,723 million
- Annualized Q1 2026 capex: $7,723 × 4 = $30,892 million
- FY2025 full year AI segment capex: $12,727 million

Now let me calculate the ratios:

1. **AWS capex-to-revenue (using consolidated capex as proxy):**
   $131,819 / $128,725 = 1.024 or ~102.4%

2. **SpaceX AI segment capex-to-revenue (annualized Q1 2026):**
   Annualized Q1 2026 capex = $7,723 × 4 = $30,892 million
   $30,892 / $3,201 = 9.65 or ~965%

   Alternatively, using FY2025 actual capex:
   $12,727 / $3,201 = 3.98 or ~398%

Let me calculate these precisely.
Now let me also retrieve information about the structural differences in the compute business models between AWS and SpaceX's AI segment.

arguments.prompt: What is the AI segment's business model, revenue model, and how does it generate revenue from compute infrastructure? What are the differences between the AI segment and traditional cloud computing in {{spacex_s1}}?
What is AWS's business model, revenue recognition, and how does AWS monetize its infrastructure in {{amazon_10k_2025}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided document excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of the AI segment's business model, revenue model, compute infrastructure monetization, and how it differs from traditional cloud computing.

### 1. Business Model
The AI segment operates a **highly vertically integrated platform** designed to achieve Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). The core thesis is that AGI will allow human cognitive capabilities to be replicated and scaled at machine speeds, which requires vast, cost-efficient, and power-efficient computational resources. 
The business model spans four main pillars:
*   **Gigawatt-Scale Compute Infrastructure:** Building and operating massive, power-efficient terrestrial (and eventually orbital) data centers.
*   **Frontier AI Models:** Developing the "truth-seeking" proprietary AI model, **Grok**.
*   **Digital & AI Solutions:** Providing AI applications and services for consumers, enterprises, and governments (e.g., Grok Business, Grok Enterprise, xAI Gov).
*   **The X Platform:** Leveraging X as a real-time information, entertainment, and free-speech platform that serves as both a distribution channel and a data source.

### 2. Revenue Model
The AI segment generates revenue primarily through digital platform services and AI solutions. Revenue streams include:
*   **Advertising:** Selling ad products on the X platform. Revenue is recognized when a user engages with the ad based on the advertiser's criteria.
*   **Subscriptions:** Premium subscriptions for X and Grok, recognized ratably over the subscription term (month-to-month up to one year).
*   **Data Licensing:** Licensing data to customers, recognized ratably as the customer consumes and benefits from the data.
*   **API Access:** Providing access to Grok models for developers and enterprises, recognized ratably for stand-ready access or on a usage-based consumption model.
*   **Enterprise & Government Solutions:** Selling specialized AI workflows and secure models to businesses and government entities.

### 3. Generating Revenue from Compute Infrastructure
While the compute infrastructure is primarily built to train proprietary models (like Grok 5) and support the X platform, the company monetizes its massive compute capacity through **strategic, selective third-party access** rather than operating as a general public cloud utility. 
*   **Cloud Services Agreements:** The company sells high-performance compute capacity to select partners. For example, a massive agreement with **Anthropic** grants them access to COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II capacity for **$1.25 billion per month** through May 2029.
*   **Strategic Partnerships & Deferred Fees:** The company enters into joint development and compute agreements, such as the partnership with **Cursor** to advance coding and knowledge AI models, which includes an **$8.5 billion deferred services fee** (payable in cash or stock).
*   **Dynamic Reallocation:** This structure allows the company to monetize unused or excess compute capacity while retaining the flexibility to reallocate resources back to internal AGI development as needed.

### 4. Differences Between the AI Segment and Traditional Cloud Computing
The text highlights several fundamental differences between the company's AI compute infrastructure and traditional cloud computing (as well as other AI companies reliant on standard terrestrial clouds):

*   **Purpose-Built for "Cost Per Token":** Traditional cloud computing focuses on general-purpose IT hosting margins. The AI segment's infrastructure economics are strictly optimized to minimize the **"cost per token"** for AI training and inference. This is driven by maximizing cluster-level throughput and minimizing data center construction, hardware, and power costs.
*   **Unprecedented Scale & Coherency:** The company is the first to deploy **coherent gigawatt-scale AI training clusters** (COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II collectively provide ~1.0 GW of compute power). Traditional cloud data centers are typically smaller, distributed, and not built as single coherent training clusters.
*   **Radical Speed and Cost of Deployment:** By repurposing existing factory shells and applying first-principles engineering, the company brought COLOSSUS online in **122 days** and COLOSSUS II in **91 days**. The industry benchmark for a traditional 100-megawatt greenfield data center is approximately **two years**. Furthermore, their construction costs per megawatt are considerably lower than industry benchmarks.
*   **Extreme Vertical Integration:** Unlike traditional cloud providers that rely on third-party hardware supply chains, the AI segment leverages cross-segment synergies. For example, the **Terafab initiative with Tesla** aims to build a manufacturing facility capable of producing 1 terawatt per year of compute hardware.
*   **Orbital AI Compute (Space-Based Solar):** Traditional cloud computing is constrained by Earth's terrestrial power grids and environmental regulations. The AI segment plans to bypass these limits by leveraging the company's Space segment (rockets and satellites) to deploy **orbital AI data centers** powered by virtually unlimited solar energy, fundamentally lowering the long-term cost structure of compute.
*   **Captive-First vs. Public Utility:** Traditional clouds (like AWS or Azure) are public utilities built to serve millions of diverse customers. The AI segment's infrastructure is a **captive, strategic asset** built first and foremost to achieve AGI and power the X platform; third-party monetization is secondary and limited to a "limited number of third-party customers" to ensure capacity is always available for internal frontier model training.

Based on the provided financial disclosures and accounting policies, here is a breakdown of AWS's business model, revenue recognition methods, and how it monetizes its infrastructure:

### 1. AWS's Business Model
AWS operates as a global cloud computing and technology services provider. Its core business model revolves around providing a broad set of on-demand technology services to a diverse customer base that includes start-ups, enterprises, government agencies, academic institutions, and developers. 
* **Service Offerings:** The portfolio includes compute, storage, database, analytics, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and other enterprise-grade IT services. 
* **Value Proposition:** Instead of customers investing in and maintaining their own physical data centers, AWS provides these resources over the cloud, allowing organizations to access scalable, cutting-edge technology on a flexible basis.

### 2. Revenue Recognition
AWS recognizes revenue based on how customers contract for and consume its services, adhering to the following accounting policies:
* **Usage-Based (On-Demand):** Revenue is primarily recognized **as the customer uses the services**. It is tied directly to the quantity of services rendered, such as the exact amount of compute hours or storage capacity delivered on-demand. Revenue is allocated to these various services using stand-alone selling prices.
* **Fixed-Term Contracts:** Certain services (including specific compute and database offerings) are sold as a fixed quantity over a specified term (e.g., Reserved Instances or committed contracts). For these arrangements, revenue is recognized **ratably** (evenly) over the life of the contract.
* **Unearned Revenue:** When payments are received in advance of performing service obligations (prepayments for AWS services), they are recorded as unearned revenue and recognized over the service period. 
* **Long-Term Performance Obligations (Backlog):** AWS holds massive commitments in customer contracts for future services. For contracts with original terms exceeding one year, these unrecognized commitments were approximately **$244 billion** as of December 31, 2025. Revenue for these will be driven by future customer usage and contractual performance.
* **Capitalized Commissions:** Sales commissions paid in connection with AWS contracts that exceed one year are capitalized and amortized over the contract term.

### 3. How AWS Monetizes its Infrastructure
AWS monetizes its massive physical and technological footprint by transforming heavy capital expenditures into high-margin, recurring revenue streams through scale and flexible pricing:
* **Shared Infrastructure Leverage:** AWS operates on a shared infrastructure model that supports both Amazon’s internal technology requirements and external sales to AWS customers. Infrastructure costs (servers, networking equipment, data center depreciation, rent, and utilities) are classified as "Technology and infrastructure" expenses.
* **Massive CapEx for Scale and AI:** Amazon continuously invests heavily in its physical footprint. The majority of the company's technology infrastructure assets and cash capital expenditures (which reached $128.3 billion consolidated in 2025) are allocated to the AWS segment to support business growth, geographic expansion, and advanced AI/ML initiatives.
* **Dual-Pricing Monetization Strategy:** 
  * *On-Demand Pricing:* AWS charges a premium for flexibility, monetizing idle or burst capacity by allowing customers to pay strictly for what they consume by the hour/second.
  * *Fixed-Term Commitments:* By offering fixed-quantity services over specified terms, AWS secures long-term, predictable revenue. This guarantees high server utilization rates and ensures that the massive upfront costs of building data centers are offset by guaranteed customer spend.
* **Asset Allocation:** The financial statements note that the vast majority of technology infrastructure assets and net additions are allocated directly to the AWS segment based on usage. This highlights that AWS is the primary engine driving Amazon's physical tech investments, effectively renting out its proprietary global hardware network to the world on a highly scalable, metered basis.

arguments.final_result:
## Capex-to-Revenue Ratio Comparison: AWS vs. SpaceX AI Segment

### 1. Verified Figures from SEC Filings

**Amazon (FY2025 10-K, filed February 6, 2026):**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AWS Net Sales (Revenue) | $128,725 million |
| Consolidated Purchases of Property & Equipment (Cash Flow Statement) | $131,819 million |
| AWS-Specific Net Additions to Property & Equipment | $96,496 million |
| AWS Segment Operating Income | $45,606 million |

**SpaceX (S-1, filed May 20, 2026 — recast consolidated entity including xAI and X Holdings):**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AI Segment Revenue (FY2025) | $3,201 million |
| AI Segment Capex (FY2025 full year) | $12,727 million |
| AI Segment Capex (Q1 2026, three months ended March 31) | $7,723 million |
| AI Segment Loss from Operations (FY2025) | $(6,355) million |
| AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA (FY2025) | $(1,237) million |

---

### 2. Capex-to-Revenue Calculations

**AWS (using consolidated Amazon capex as proxy, per the question's instruction):**

$$\frac{\$131{,}819\text{M}}{\$128{,}725\text{M}} = 1.02\text{x} \quad (102.4\%)$$

*For reference, using AWS-specific capex only:*

$$\frac{\$96{,}496\text{M}}{\$128{,}725\text{M}} = 0.75\text{x} \quad (75.0\%)$$

**SpaceX AI Segment (annualized Q1 2026 capex):**

Annualized Q1 2026 capex = $7,723M × 4 = **$30,892 million**

$$\frac{\$30{,}892\text{M}}{\$3{,}201\text{M}} = 9.65\text{x} \quad (965\%)$$

*For reference, using FY2025 actual full-year AI capex:*

$$\frac{\$12{,}727\text{M}}{\$3{,}201\text{M}} = 3.98\text{x} \quad (398\%)$$

---

### 3. Summary Table

| Metric | AWS (Consolidated Proxy) | SpaceX AI (Annualized Q1 '26) | SpaceX AI (FY2025 Actual) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $128.7B | $3.2B | $3.2B |
| Capex | $131.8B | $30.9B (annualized) | $12.7B |
| **Capex / Revenue** | **1.02x** | **9.65x** | **3.98x** |
| Operating Income/(Loss) | $45.6B (35.4% margin) | N/A (FY: $(6.4B) loss) | $(6.4B) loss |

---

### 4. What the Comparison Implies About Infrastructure Buildout vs. Monetization Maturity

The ~9.5x gap between the two ratios (9.65x vs. 1.02x) is analytically stark and reflects fundamentally different positions on the infrastructure-investment lifecycle:

**AWS — Mature Monetization Phase:**
AWS's capex-to-revenue ratio of ~1.02x (consolidated) or ~0.75x (AWS-specific) indicates that its infrastructure spending is approximately covered by current-period revenue. AWS generated $45.6 billion in operating income on $128.7 billion in revenue (a 35.4% operating margin), demonstrating that its infrastructure buildout has already achieved scale-efficient monetization. AWS is investing for growth and AI capacity expansion, but from a position of proven, profitable unit economics. Its $244 billion in remaining performance obligations (backlog) further confirms deep revenue visibility against which capex is being deployed.

**SpaceX AI Segment — Pre-Revenue-Scale Infrastructure Buildout:**
The AI segment's annualized capex-to-revenue ratio of 9.65x means it is spending nearly **$10 on infrastructure for every $1 of revenue** it generates. Even using FY2025 actual capex, the ratio was 3.98x. The S-1 explicitly describes the segment as being in an "earlier stage of development" operating in a "nascent and rapidly evolving market" with a "multi-year investment horizon" before achieving sustained positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA. The segment reported a $(6,355) million operating loss and $(1,237) million Adjusted EBITDA in FY2025. The Q1 2026 capex acceleration (from $2,567M in Q1 2025 to $7,723M in Q1 2026, a 3x increase) signals that the buildout is intensifying well ahead of revenue scaling.

**Implication:** AWS is in a *harvest-and-expand* phase where capex is funded by and proportionate to an established revenue base. SpaceX's AI segment is in a *build-then-monetize* phase where capex is massively front-loaded relative to current revenue, with monetization expected to follow infrastructure deployment by multiple years.

---

### 5. Structural Difference in Compute-Business Models: Why a Direct Multiple Comparison Is Analytically Incomplete

A direct capex-to-revenue multiple comparison between AWS and SpaceX's AI segment is analytically incomplete because of a fundamental structural difference in how each entity's compute business operates:

**AWS operates a public cloud utility model (infrastructure-as-a-service):**
- Revenue is generated by selling generalized, on-demand compute, storage, and services to millions of diverse customers on a metered (usage-based) or committed-term basis.
- Infrastructure is a shared, multi-tenant platform where capex is directly and continuously converted into billable service capacity.
- The capex-to-revenue ratio reflects a relatively tight feedback loop: deploy servers → customers consume → revenue flows.
- Revenue scales roughly proportionally with infrastructure deployment.

**SpaceX's AI segment operates a captive-first, vertically integrated AGI-development model:**
- Infrastructure is built **primarily to train proprietary frontier models** (Grok) and power the X platform — not to serve as a general-purpose public cloud.
- Third-party monetization is **secondary and selective**: the S-1 discloses that compute is sold to a "limited number of third-party customers" (e.g., the $1.25B/month Anthropic agreement, the $8.5B Cursor deferred services arrangement) to ensure capacity remains available for internal AGI development.
- Revenue streams are heterogeneous and not purely compute-consumption-based: they include advertising on X, consumer/enterprise subscriptions (X Premium, Grok), data licensing, and API access — many of which are not directly proportional to capex deployed.
- The segment's infrastructure economics are optimized for **"cost per token"** of AI training and inference, not for maximizing third-party revenue per dollar of capex.

**Why this makes direct comparison incomplete:**
1. **Revenue is not the correct denominator for SpaceX's AI capex.** A significant portion of SpaceX's AI infrastructure spending generates value through proprietary model capabilities (Grok), platform enhancement (X), and strategic positioning — value that does not flow through the revenue line in the way AWS's infrastructure spend does.
2. **AWS's consolidated capex includes non-AWS spending** (~$35.9B for North America, ~$7.6B International, ~$2.3B Corporate), making the consolidated proxy an overstatement of AWS-specific infrastructure intensity.
3. **Timing mismatch:** SpaceX's Q1 2026 capex reflects an acceleration phase (COLOSSUS II deployment in 91 days), while AWS's FY2025 capex reflects steady-state expansion of a 20-year-old platform.
4. **Revenue composition differs fundamentally:** AWS revenue is almost entirely infrastructure-consumption-driven; SpaceX AI revenue includes advertising, subscriptions, and licensing that are not direct functions of compute capacity deployed.

A more analytically complete comparison would require normalizing for: (a) the proportion of capex dedicated to revenue-generating vs. internal-R&D infrastructure, (b) the expected monetization timeline and terminal revenue capacity of deployed infrastructure, and (c) the non-revenue strategic value of proprietary model development embedded in SpaceX's capex.

---

### Sources

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1018724/000101872426000004/amzn-20251231.htm",
      "name": "Amazon.com, Inc. FY2025 10-K (filed February 6, 2026)"
    }
  ]
